title: The Money Story That's Costing Women Millions (And How to Rewrite It)
guestBio: Stephanie Guttman is the founder of Huntress Wealth, a financial wellness and planning firm dedicated to helping women reclaim confidence with their finances. She hosts the Talk Rich to Me podcast and authors The Arrow, a weekly newsletter helping women align their money with their values.
excerpt: Your relationship with money was shaped before you could tie your shoes—and those invisible scripts are costing women more than the wage gap ever could. Financial expert Stephanie Guttman reveals how to identify and rewrite the money story that's holding you back.
metaDescription: Discover the 4 money stories formed by age 7 that shape your financial decisions. Learn why the investing gap costs women more than the wage gap.
keyTakeaways: Your money story is formed by age 7 through interactions with parents, peers, and trusted adults, creating invisible scripts that drive your financial behavior.
The four core money stories are avoidance, money worship, status, and vigilance—with vigilance being the only one correlated with positive financial outcomes.
The investing gap costs women far more long-term than the wage gap because compound interest doubles your money roughly every 10 years.
ETFs allow you to invest in hundreds of stocks with a single purchase, making investing accessible without needing to beat the market.
Teaching children about investing before age 16 triples their likelihood of becoming confident investors as adults.
Women receive only 2-3% of all available capital, making it crucial for women to start investing in women-led businesses and startups.
Financial agency begins with teaching children to make real money decisions, not just theoretical lessons about saving.

pullQuotes.text: The investing gap is costing women far more than the wage gap, and the only way to close it is to stop waiting and start investing.
Your money story was shaped before you could even tie your shoes. What if the beliefs holding you back aren't even yours?
Kids who learn about investing before 16 are 3x more likely to be confident investors as adults.

timestamps.label: The Outsider's Perspective: Why Stephanie Entered Finance
The Investing Gap vs. The Wage Gap
Money Stories: The Beliefs Formed by Age 7
The Four Core Money Stories Explained
Amy's Money Story: Bank of Amy
Teaching Kids About Money & Financial Agency
ETFs Explained: One Purchase, Whole Market
Talking to Kids About Investing Before Age 16
Closing: Managing Your Money is Managing Your Power

content:
What if I told you that your relationship with money—every financial decision you make, every investment you avoid, every dollar you save or spend—was largely determined before you learned to tie your shoes? It sounds impossible, but according to financial expert Stephanie Guttman, founder of Huntress Wealth, this is exactly what's happening to millions of women every day.

In this transformative episode of The Manage Her, Stephanie reveals a truth that will make you rethink everything you thought you knew about your finances: your money story is costing you more than you could ever imagine, and it's time to rewrite the script.

The Four Money Stories That Rule Your Life

By age seven, you had already absorbed one of four core money stories from your parents, peers, and trusted adults. These invisible scripts have been running your financial show ever since, and most women have no idea they exist. Stephanie breaks down the four money stories that shape our adult financial behavior: avoidance, money worship, status, and vigilance.

The avoidance story teaches you that money is the root of all evil, that wealthy people are somehow morally corrupt, and that it's better to just not think about money at all. If this sounds familiar, you might find yourself literally avoiding financial conversations, putting off investment decisions, or feeling physically uncomfortable when discussing money.

Money worship flips the script entirely, creating the belief that money will solve all your problems and bring you happiness. Women with this story often find themselves in cycles of earning and spending, always chasing the next financial high that will finally make everything perfect.

The status story ties your self-worth directly to your net worth. Your financial decisions become less about building wealth and more about maintaining appearances, often leading to lifestyle inflation that keeps you from true financial security.

But here's where it gets interesting: only one of these four money stories is actually correlated with positive financial outcomes. Vigilance—the belief that financial security requires constant attention and careful planning—is the money story that builds wealth. However, even vigilance has a dark side when it becomes obsessive saving without strategic investing.

Why the Investing Gap Trumps the Wage Gap

While we've spent decades talking about the wage gap, Stephanie presents a startling reality: the investing gap is costing women far more than unequal pay ever could. When women avoid investing and instead keep their money in savings accounts earning minimal interest, they're missing out on the magic of compound growth that could double their money every ten years.

This isn't about becoming a day trader or mastering complex financial instruments. Stephanie explains how ETFs (Exchange-Traded Funds) make investing accessible to everyone. With a single purchase, you can own hundreds of stocks across entire markets. You don't need to beat the market; you just need to participate in it.

The numbers are staggering when you consider compound interest over time. That money sitting in your savings account could be working exponentially harder for you in the market. While there's always risk involved, the historical data shows that long-term market participation has been one of the most reliable paths to wealth building.

Breaking the Cycle: Teaching the Next Generation

Perhaps the most powerful part of this conversation centers on how we can break these limiting money stories for our children. Stephanie shares research showing that children who learn about investing before age 16 are three times more likely to become confident investors as adults. This isn't about creating little financial sharks; it's about giving them the tools to make empowered decisions about their futures.

The key is moving beyond theoretical lessons about saving to creating real experiences with financial agency. When children make actual money decisions—even small ones—they develop the confidence and skills they'll need as adults. Stephanie advocates for using tools like debit cards designed for kids, allowing them to experience the connection between their choices and their financial reality.

But it goes deeper than just teaching mechanics. We need to model healthy relationships with money. When we avoid financial conversations, make money decisions from a place of fear, or treat investing as something only other people do, we're passing down our limiting stories to the next generation.

The Systemic Challenge We Can't Ignore

While personal money stories matter enormously, Stephanie also addresses the systemic issues at play. Women currently receive only 2-3% of all available capital, which means the vast majority of funding, investment, and financial opportunity flows to men. This isn't just unfair; it's economically inefficient and represents a massive missed opportunity.

The solution isn't just waiting for systems to change. Women need to start investing in women-led businesses and startups. We need to participate in the markets that have historically excluded us. Every dollar women invest in other women creates a ripple effect that challenges these systemic inequalities.

Your Money Story Starts Today

The most liberating part of understanding your money story is realizing that you have the power to rewrite it. Those beliefs formed when you were seven years old don't have to dictate your financial future. Whether your story is rooted in avoidance, worship, status, or even productive vigilance, you can consciously choose new beliefs that serve your goals.

This means getting honest about your current relationship with money. What emotions come up when you think about investing? What stories do you tell yourself about why you're not ready, not qualified, or not worthy of financial success? These are clues to the invisible scripts that have been running your financial life.

As Stephanie powerfully states, managing your money is managing your power. When women take control of their financial lives, when they stop waiting and start investing, when they pass down empowering money stories to their children, they don't just change their own lives—they change the entire economic landscape.

Your seven-year-old self absorbed the money stories that were available to her then. But your adult self gets to choose what comes next. The question isn't whether you're ready to invest or whether you know enough about finance. The question is: what story do you want your money to tell about your life?

quiz.title: What's Your Money Story?
quiz.description: Discover the invisible financial script that's been running your life since childhood and learn how to rewrite it for success.

questions.question: When you think about your financial future, what's your honest first reaction?

options.text: I feel a little anxious and prefer to focus on more immediate concerns
I get excited thinking about all the possibilities money could create
I think about what financial success would say about me as a person
I start running through my budget and savings plan in my head

questions.question: Your friend just got a huge promotion and salary increase. Your first thought is:

options.text: I hope all that money doesn't change who they are
I wonder how I can position myself for a similar opportunity
I'm excited for them—they've earned that level of success
I hope they're putting some of that increase toward their retirement fund

questions.question: When it comes to investing, you tend to:

options.text: Feel overwhelmed by all the options and put off making decisions
Look for investments that could really accelerate your wealth building
Consider what kinds of investments successful people in your field make
Research thoroughly and choose stable, long-term options

questions.question: At a dinner party, when the conversation turns to money, you:

options.text: Try to steer the conversation toward more comfortable topics
Share your latest financial wins or strategies you're excited about
Listen carefully to gauge where others stand financially
Engage thoughtfully but don't reveal too much about your own situation
That focusing too much on money will make me lose sight of what really matters
That I'll never have enough to live the life I really want
That people will judge me based on my financial situation
That I'll make a costly mistake or run out of money in retirement

questions.question: When you make a significant purchase, you feel best when:

options.text: It's something practical that you really need and will use for years
It's an investment in your future success or happiness
It's something that reflects your values and where you are in life
You've planned for it and it fits comfortably within your budget

results.title: The Money Avoider
results.description: You've learned to see money as complicated or even morally questionable, so you prefer to keep financial decisions simple and avoid the stress altogether. Your heart is in the right place—you value relationships and authenticity over material wealth. However, this avoidance might be costing you long-term financial security. This episode will show you how to transform your relationship with money from something you avoid into a tool for creating the life you truly want, without compromising your values.

results.title: The Wealth Chaser
results.description: You believe deeply that financial success will unlock the life you're meant to live, and you're willing to work hard to get there. Your drive and ambition are real strengths that can take you far. Sometimes though, you might find yourself on an endless treadmill of earning and spending, always chasing the next level. This episode will help you channel your natural motivation into sustainable wealth-building strategies that actually deliver the security and freedom you're seeking.

results.title: The Status Strategist
results.description: You understand that money represents more than just numbers—it's about respect, influence, and having a seat at the table. Your awareness of how wealth creates opportunities is sophisticated and valuable. You might sometimes find yourself making financial choices based on how they look rather than how they serve your long-term goals. This episode will show you how to leverage your natural understanding of money's power while building genuine wealth that matches your impressive exterior.

results.title: The Financial Guardian
results.description: You approach money with wisdom and caution, understanding that financial security requires discipline and smart planning. Your natural tendency toward vigilance is actually your superpower—research shows it's the only money story correlated with positive financial outcomes. You sometimes worry that you're too conservative or that you should be taking bigger risks. This episode will validate your approach while showing you practical ways to grow your wealth confidently and securely.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Welcome back to The Manager podcast, the show where we turn invisible work into visible leadership. I'm your host, Aimee Rickabus. Today, we're talking about money - not spreadsheets and stress, but mindset, story, and self-worth. My guest, Stephanie Guttman, is the founder of Huntress Wealth, a financial wellness and planning firm helping women reclaim confidence with their finances.

Stephanie Guttman came into personal finance from the outside. After years in corporate finance, she was determined to understand what truly motivates women when it comes to wealth and how investing can become a source of freedom rather than fear. That outsider's perspective became her superpower. Through Huntress Wealth, she's built an ecosystem that blends investing education, financial therapy, and mindset transformation. Her goal? To get more women investing, not just saving, and to heal the old money stories that keep us small.

She's also the host of Talk Rich to Me, and the author of a weekly newsletter called The Arrow, where she helps women aim their money toward meaning. Stephanie, welcome to The Manager. Let's talk about what happens when women start managing their wealth with intention.

**Stephanie:** Thank you so much for having me, Aimee. So glad to be on The Manager.

**Aimee:** So you've said you entered finance from the outside. You weren't groomed by Wall Street, but you were drawn in by curiosity and math. Could you share that journey? What did you see from the outside that made you want to change how women experience finance?

**Stephanie:** I studied finance in undergrad, went into corporate finance, have been in fintechs for the last several years, but really have not touched this personal finance space. I was just so wanted to crack why women didn't hold equal power. And what I got to was that we don't hold equal money. And so I really, really wanted to understand why we didn't.

And we all know the stories of the pay gap. But there's a much broader story of the investing gap. And I just could not not spend all my time figuring out how to help women close it.

**Aimee:** I love that. I love that so much. So you shared something fascinating. Our money story is formed by the age of seven and influenced by both our family and our peers. Could you unpack that?

**Stephanie:** Money stories are this idea - there's a whole financial psychology world out there that is super fun to spend time in. And money stories are kind of one pillar of that. And these are the things that we start telling ourselves from a very young age. And we do learn them from peers. We learn them from parents. We learn them from other trusted adults. And they really just help you kind of categorize whether you view money as good or bad.

And there's four core stories. One is avoidance - maybe money stresses you out anytime. You don't want to look at it or you feel like people who have a lot of it are greedy. So it's like money bad. You're avoiding it. There's money worship where you think money will solve every problem. And so if you get a raise, you deserve it, go celebrate and spend it all. Or you've had a bad day, spending that money will make you feel better. And so it's just using money to solve your problems is money worship.

Then there's status where you're equating your self-worth with how much you have. So your self-worth equals your net worth. And so it's like, if I have more money, I'm a better person, but we all know that doesn't necessarily make you happier or a better person. So that's a really big catch 22 as well.

And then the last one is vigilance. And so this is Money is what keeps me safe. If I'm disciplined, I will be okay. And so that's where you get into really frugality and diet type behaviors when it comes to money. And so unfortunately, we can have more than one story, but we are exposed to these kinds of things through people around us when we're really young. And that kind of becomes our relationship to money.

**Aimee:** How interesting. Could you give me an example of a healthy story about money versus an unhealthy one?

**Stephanie:** Yes. I will say of those four where they all sound bad, right? It's okay for those to be your story where there's like - I was thinking of someone who I really appreciate who always refers to things as attics or basements. Right. And so there's a version where you might have that story, but it helps you. Right. And so you cut out, you're not thinking the worst parts of that story. But those positive things are reinforcing. Right. And then there's the basement where your behavior is really, really over dialed and it's not helping you.

But of those four stories, vigilance is actually correlated with good financial outcomes because you save a lot more. That doesn't mean your mental health is 100 percent necessarily, but your bank account might be really strong where the other three are associated with lower credit growth in your assets. And so what is good? It's when they're really, really over dialed that they're bad.

**Aimee:** Got it. Very interesting. That's so interesting. How do these stories show up in adulthood? I guess would be a good follow up to that one.

**Stephanie:** Well, I feel like I should ask you, Aimee, how do they?

**Aimee:** Oh my gosh. Seriously, right?

**Stephanie:** I think that's, you know, you mentioned the podcast Talk Rich to me, and that's what we love to do on there is have women come on and tell their stories that are life stories and financial stories, because you do start to see these dots that connect. For me, I'm definitely, I grew up with vigilance, right? And so again, that set me on a really good path in my early career. I was saving a whole lot, but maybe not spending money to take care of myself in that holistic way that I've worked on and I do more of now. I think it shows up for everyone slightly differently. But if you start to look for these patterns, you will definitely find them.

**Aimee:** Oh, totally. Yeah. My parents called me Bank of Amy when I was a kid, B of A, because they gave me this little leather sack when I was probably about two years old. And I would carry my money around in it. They called me money bags. And so I guess vigilance is definitely part of my money story from a very young age. I've always had at least $500 in the bank. That's just sort of, even from the time I was five years old.

**Stephanie:** Wow. I love the idea of you walking around with bags of money. Maybe bringing that back would be a healthy part of that behavior. I don't know. It sounds really fun.

**Aimee:** Oh, I still do actually. I mean, let me pull my purse out.

**Stephanie:** Does it make you feel safe, Aimee?

**Aimee:** I guess it does. Yeah. And it gets the babysitters paid.

**Stephanie:** There you go. Convenience for sure.

**Aimee:** I just noticed more people are taking money now too. So if I go get my hair done or I get my nails done, the girls like to get paid in cash. And it's good for our financial system to pay for things in cash because that way we keep cash in play. Otherwise, we'll lose cash and go full digital. So we do use cash.

**Stephanie:** I hadn't thought about doing my part on that front. I was going to say, we keep cash around mostly because I have a seven-year-old at home. And so you transact with kids in cash because they do not understand digital. So our whole cash system is in ones, in fives so we can work with the kids.

**Aimee:** When your kiddo gets a little bigger, the Greenlight card is actually really cool. My older kids have Greenlight cards. So their allowance goes on their Greenlight cards. They can use it when they're riding their bikes around. They want to stop and get some bagel at the bagel shack or buy some bait at the bait and tackle or whatever they're doing. So I love that they have that sense of freedom and agency with their money.

**Stephanie:** I do. I like that. It's really important to train kids with money so that they can see what it's like to be an adult a little bit because we got to keep the training wheels on them. But I was talking to Amanda Jaggard on another episode and we were talking about how you're not raising children, you're raising adults.

**Aimee:** Oh, yeah. That's one of my mottos. Yeah. Yeah. And I think my parents were always really good about that with me, making sure I had money to have agency over money from a very young age. And I think that that's one of the things I really wanted my kids to have too.

**Stephanie:** Same. And I think for me, what I'm really cautious about is not just teaching spending and saving, but I opened a custodial account with a small amount of money for my daughter and turned paper statements on. All paper statements are off for everything else, but for hers, we get the paper statements. So every month we can sit down and see what happened together. And it's like, she will learn investing.

**Aimee:** That's so cool. Yeah. My 16 year old is really into it. He's like, Hey mom, how much money is in my account? because he can't touch it, but he can grow it. And so he's got $17,938 in his account today. I love, I do. I love money. I think money is freedom. I think money is opportunity. For me, I don't know if he'll end up going to college. He's not that kind of kid, but he really wants to start his own business. And this is money. This is seed money for him to be an entrepreneur. So I'm looking forward to growing that for him over the next four years, because I think he'll probably want to launch his company around between 20 and 22.

**Stephanie:** I can see him launching a company. Interesting. Follow along. Stay tuned.

**Aimee:** Stay tuned. Yeah, totally. He's my first one out the gate, besides my stepdaughter, who's 27. But I'm excited to see what Julian does because he's always been very good with money. And yeah, he's like, Mom, can I invest in crypto? And I'm like, hmm, we should look at that. Let's talk about that, right?

**Stephanie:** Yeah. Yeah, we should look at that.

**Aimee:** So you often say that mindset is the real problem, not math. I can see that. How do you help women bridge the gap between knowing what to do and believing that they can do it?

**Stephanie:** Oh, there you go. That's the money questioning. How do we do that? I think, especially when we're talking about learning as adults, right? People are familiar with the growth mindset and all of that. But what it comes down to is just doing. And I mean a little bit of admit that you're scared and do it anyway. But just start acting. And it can be little small steps that don't have big consequences at first, right? But practice engaging with your money, checking your accounts regularly, see what it feels like, right? Bring up money with a friend, see how it goes. Keep trying that if it feels good, right? The way to help get over mindset is to just do it anyway, right? Start engaging anyway. And then your confidence just, it's just a steady path. It grows and it compounds. And then you're comfortable doing bigger and bigger things.

**Aimee:** Totally. In LA, we call that fake it till you make it.

**Stephanie:** Oh yeah, for sure. It really helps. I think there's this notion that everybody else has it all figured out too, that's a common story that we tell ourselves and we don't. Money is a made up construct and it's a system that you can learn and you can do it, right? Everybody's had to learn this. No one is born knowing it. There is a little bit of a fake it till you make it, but it's just that notion that the emperor's not wearing clothes. Nobody really knows what they're doing until they do. And everybody's had to go on this journey.

**Aimee:** Oh yeah. Yeah. Everybody's learning all the time, but my mom always said no money, no fun. And I think that made money fun for me. I think that one of my ideas of money is that it can bring fun into your life because of my mom putting that in my head at such a young age.

**Stephanie:** One of my favorite, one of the providers that we work with, one of the wellness providers, one of her favorite questions with people is okay, you walk into a room and you have this relationship with money. You see money there. Who is money or what does money look like? And so when you're saying money is fun, right? I'm picturing what fun money as a person or other concept would look like in that spot. But it's really visualizing that relationship. So I love hearing fun. Fun is really fun to do that with.

**Aimee:** Well, I think for me, my mindset has always been kind of one of curiosity and adventure. So I try to approach most things in life that way. Because if I'm doing something new and something that's scary, if I'm doing it with that spirit of curiosity, that spirit of adventure, it makes it fun. Yeah. What was the word you just taught me before we got on? Spiritualpreneur?

**Stephanie:** Oh, spiritualpreneur was the word. I was working with ChatGPT and we were working on some copy for the new website for themanager.com. And ChatGPT's like, oh, you're a spiritualpreneur. And I'm like, oh, I like that. Because most entrepreneurs who run big companies - Tomahawk is going to do $100 million in revenue this year, which is woo-woo. But most of them, I've been a Reiki master for 20 years, and I'm a yogi, and my joy and my happiness are way more of a priority to me and my life than the amount of money that I have in the bank. I like having money in the bank. It's cool. It's fun. You do cool stuff with it. But if you're not all around happy, it's just a number with a bunch of zeros behind it.

**Aimee:** Yeah, I really latched on to it when you said it, not just because it's a fun word, but also because it's the whole basis of what I hope for people with Huntress is knowing what your values are and using money as a tool to enact them. Right. And that's very much so when you've decided to start a company, what you were deciding to do. It might not be starting a company. It could be something else, but stepping into your power and shouting your values out loud.

**Stephanie:** When I stepped into the CEO position at Tomahawk, four months later, I was put in a board seat for a very prominent nonprofit that I'm very passionate about. And I immediately knew I wanted to grow this company so that I would have the money to donate to the nonprofits that I wanted to donate to. And I was like, I want to donate $100,000 a year to my nonprofits that I believe in. And that was sort of what got me going. And I think having that purpose-driven mindset, a purpose bigger than just a fancy car or a fancy house is so important for your why.

**Aimee:** Yeah. It's funny you say versus fancy car or fancy house. I kind of don't care what people's why is. Those are even okay too if that house symbolizes where you can gather your family and your community and it's really chasing. There's not a right and wrong way to do this. It's just, really, really knowing what those values are, right?

**Stephanie:** Well, knowing what you want is so good. And being able to visualize them. My husband and I were always huge on doing vision boards. We've been married for 14 years. We'll be 14 in December. And 14 years ago, we didn't have that much. I was kind of starting over after the recession. I was in real estate. I was a real estate investor. So I was kind of starting from scratch. I think I had maybe $50,000 left in the bank, which is not a lot for me. And it was start from scratch time.

And so we would break out our vision boards and get our heads around, okay, I want to live here and I want this car and I want to build this company and I want to do these kinds of numbers. And having an idea of where you want to go really helps you get there.

**Aimee:** It's been talking with a lot of women when we were shaping what Huntress was going to look like. I thought it was just going to be this shiny tech app that everyone goes downloads and all their problems are solved. Yeah. And I still hope it's that, we are building that, but the mental part of it is way, way stronger than I thought. And again, that power of knowing what you want is so important. And it's not just doing the math. And so I want to see your vision boards.

**Stephanie:** I'll take pictures. I'll send them to you. It's so impressive. But the craziest thing is we'll go back and find our old vision boards and we're like, oh my God, we own that car exactly. The same car, same color, same year that was in the picture. And we're like, what?

**Aimee:** That's so good. Without intentionally doing that, it's amazing.

**Stephanie:** It's the power of the subconscious mind. You want to take those subconscious ideas and then bring them up to consciousness, at least for a moment. They can go back to their subconscious, but your conscious brain picked up, okay, this is where we want to go. I love those aspects of making money in the financial. I love your financial therapy meets financial planning thing you've got going on.

**Aimee:** So your membership model includes talk therapists, hypnotherapists, and educators. That's unusual in finance. What made you combine those worlds and what transformations have you seen because of it?

**Stephanie:** Yeah, I should give the caveat that our talk therapists and hypnotherapists are those in their day jobs. They're licensed for those, but they're not providing therapy for Huntress. That implies diagnosis and plan. And so it's really this mindset and wellness and in the realm of money and what you really want out of life, your values, helping surface what's important.

And so some people can go and vision board on their own, but other people really, really need help and support, recognizing either what stories might be holding them back or what really truly is most important to them. Again, I didn't think we'd start there. But in talking with women, it's just so common that we don't let ourselves do that. Right. We're either afraid of what might come up or we think we might not ever be able to attain what we want. Right. And so there's this fear, anxiety, sometimes shame and guilt around it. It's really hard to tackle that on your own sometimes.

And so we did decide to have this service component pulling together these amazing providers because it is really just hard to do on your own sometimes.

**Aimee:** It really is. I've used the hypnotherapist. My husband and I had a success coach. Gosh, when I decided I wanted to write my book, I had started writing the book and then kind of didn't really know exactly, new world, totally out of my wheelhouse. And we brought in a success coach to work with both of us. And it's amazing now when I look at my life, all of the things that I'm doing that we worked on in our sessions, the podcast, and I'm now writing my second book and building the community and building the website and going out and talking on other people's shows.

And it's so cool that what you could do with a hypnotherapist is pretty impressive if you haven't worked with one. I definitely suggest you give it a try.

**Stephanie:** Very much so. It was something I was a little skeptical on because I just had the media version of hypnotherapy. I didn't actually know in real life. And so when I was connected, the person that we work with, her name is Julie Shui. She's amazing. And when I was connected with her and learned what it was actually like and what it can actually do for you, it's really just helping surface what's deep inside. That's all it is, right? She's like, no, I can't make you bark like a dog if you don't want to bark like a dog. It's not about planting ideas. It's about surfacing ideas. And that was amazing. My mind has been opened since I've been on this journey.

**Aimee:** That's amazing. Could you give me some examples of transformations that you've seen in your practice with this?

**Stephanie:** Yeah. One of my favorites is a lot of people do choose to start with our visualizing wealth session, which is a session that Julie and we now have another provider who can also do that session with us. And this is really just imagining your future state, trying to surface those wants and desires, right? And then giving shape and color and feeling to what is in your way so you can help chart your path. And so that's what this session is.

There's someone who went through it who she was like, I'm not a woo woo person, but it does feel like I think I have this scarcity mindset and I really need to work on it. So I'll give it a shot. Right. And after her session, she's like, yeah, so I said a lot of things because I felt like I was supposed to say them. But then I really reflected on why I was saying them. Right. Why did I feel like I was supposed to say those things?

And so she had admitted basically that she wasn't entirely truthful with herself or the provider in that session. And her big realization was she doesn't actually have scarcity mindset. She just had almost this fear that she couldn't do it. And then she's like, but I can do it. Logically, I know I can do this. So I'm just going to do it.

That one's a little indirect, but she had a chunk of her portfolio that she was holding in cash and she knew she shouldn't be. But she had this fear of acting on it. And then every time she went to, she stopped herself. And it's now fully invested according to her own asset allocation that she came up with that we gave her all the resources to help make this decision. So when? I can go point to that one. She just started doing it. And she started doing it bigger.
